unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3! In this unit you will learn all about time and daily life. You will name the twelve months of the year and put them in order. You will talk about your daily routine — what you do at each hour of the day. You will learn to talk about the past (what already happened), the present (what happens every day), and the future (what will happen soon). By the end, you will be able to make your own calendar, just like Amal does in this unit’s story, “The Calendar on the Wall”.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary section. Learn a few months and time words each day.
Work through the six short Grammar lessons, one lesson a day.
Read the story “The Calendar on the Wall” and answer the Comprehension questions.
Work through Reading and listening, then Speaking, then Writing, then Activities.
Finish with the Quiz and the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: A cartoon child marking the months on a wall calendar beside a round clock

vocabularyGroups.title: Months of the Year (January to June)
Months of the Year (July to December)

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (January to June)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The first month of the year.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The school year begins in January.
practiceSentences: Amal wrote her first goal on the January page.
The new calendar starts with January at the top.
What do you look forward to in January?
Sami said, "January mornings are cool and fresh."
My reading diary begins on the first of January.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: J - a - n - u - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, name the month after January, then ask the tutor to check your January sentence.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (January to June)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The second month of the year, and the shortest with twenty-eight days.
practiceSentences: Every fourth February gains one extra day on the calendar.
February has twenty-eight days in most years.
Which day in February is your school fair?
Maya said, "February passed so quickly this year."
My aunt's wedding was in February last year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: F - e - b - r - u - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper to listen while the tutor quizzes you on why February is the shortest month.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (January to June)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The third month of the year, when heavy rains often arrive.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The rains often come in March.
practiceSentences: Strong winds shake the mango trees all through March.
March sits between February and April on the calendar.
Does your school hold sports practice in March?
Theo said, "The farmers plant maize in March."
My kite flew highest on a windy March afternoon.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say what the weather is like in March where you live, then ask the tutor to correct your sentence.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (January to June)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The fourth month of the year, when flowers bloom in many places.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We plant our garden in April.
practiceSentences: The garden beds are turned over early in April.
Gentle April showers help the seedlings grow tall.
Is the book fair in April this year?
Nora said, "April is the greenest month here."
My family picnics in the park every April.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor two things that grow in April, with an adult listening to your pronunciation.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (January to June)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The fifth month of the year, which comes between April and June.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My cousin was born in May.
practiceSentences: Warm evenings in May draw the whole class outside.
May is the fifth month on the calendar.
Does the mango season start in May?
Adam said, "My birthday party is in May."
My baby sister was born early in May.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to help you name one family event in May, then say the date aloud with your helper.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (January to June)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The sixth month of the year, often warm and sunny.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In June, we have school games.
practiceSentences: The sports field is busiest during the last week of June.
School games fill the sunny days of June.
Will the term finish at the end of June?
Sami said, "June afternoons are perfect for football."
My cousins visit us every June holiday.
dictionaryLinks.sentenceStarter: By the end of June, I
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With a grown-up nearby, describe one school activity that happens in June and ask for feedback.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (July to December)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The seventh month of the year, often very warm.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We go to visit family in July.
practiceSentences: Idris fills his water bottle twice on hot July mornings.
July is the seventh month, right after June.
Do you travel to the village in July?
Maya said, "July is when we visit Grandma."
My favourite melon ripens in hot July.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor which months are hottest where you live, then compare July with your own answer.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (July to December)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The eighth month of the year, and a common time for family travel.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In August, we go on vacation to visit Grandma Hana.
practiceSentences: Bags are packed the night before the August journey.
August comes after July and before September.
Is it very hot here in August?
Theo said, "We repaint the classroom in August."
My family picks dates from the palms in August.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: A - u - g - u - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say three sentences that begin with In August, and ask your helper to check the capital letter.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (July to December)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The ninth month of the year, when school often starts again.
practiceSentences: Fresh timetables go up on the wall each September.
New pencils and books arrive in September.
Does the new class list come out in September?
Leo said, "September means meeting our new teacher."
My school shoes are always shiny in September.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: S - e - p - t - e - m - b - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to test whether you can spell September, with an adult checking your written answer.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (July to December)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The tenth month of the year, when many fruits are picked.
practiceSentences: The class counts down the days left in October.
October brings ripe mangoes to the market stalls.
Is the harvest fair in October this year?
Adam said, "October evenings are cool and calm."
My grandmother dries fruit every October.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: O - c - t - o - b - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Describe what your family picks or buys in October, then ask the tutor for one better word.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (July to December)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The eleventh month of the year, after October and before December.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The school year ends in November.
practiceSentences: Amal tidies her desk on the last day of November.
November is the eleventh month of the year.
Do exams finish by the end of November?
Sami said, "November means the school year is ending."
My report card comes home in November.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: N - o - v - e - m - b - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor what comes after November, then tell your helper how the school year ends.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Months of the Year (July to December)
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The twelfth and last month of the year.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In December, the weather is hot here.
practiceSentences: Idris circles the last school day in December.
December closes the year as the twelfth month.
Is it hot here in December?
Maya said, "December holidays are for visiting family."
My family cleans the whole house in December.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: D - e - c - e - m - b - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, count from January to December aloud and ask the tutor to time you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A paper or digital tool that shows the days, weeks and months of a year.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We use a calendar to know what day it is.
practiceSentences: Grandma Hana keeps a small calendar beside the kitchen door.
The calendar shows twelve months in neat rows.
Can you find Friday on the calendar?
Theo said, "I marked sports day on our calendar."
My calendar has a picture for every month.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - l - e - n - d - a - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Show your helper a real calendar, point to today, and ask the tutor to check what you say.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A length of time that lasts sixty minutes.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are 24 hours in one day.
practiceSentences: Teacher Yasmin gives the class one hour for the project.
One hour of reading passes quickly with a good book.
How many minutes are in one hour?
Mum said, "Dinner will be ready in one hour."
I play outside for an hour after homework.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor how many minutes make one hour, then tell your helper what you do each hour.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A period of one hundred years.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A century ago, people had no printed calendars.
practiceSentences: Amal drew a timeline showing one whole century.
A century is one hundred years long.
What was school like a century ago?
Grandpa said, "Our old fort is over a century old."
I wonder what homes will look like next century.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - e - n - t - u - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult what your town looked like long ago, then ask the tutor to explain a century.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A time when you stop school or work to rest and have fun.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I am going to visit Grandma Hana for my vacation.
practiceSentences: Idris counts the days until the long vacation begins.
The family packed early for their village vacation.
Where will you go for your vacation?
Daniel said, "My best vacation was by the sea."
I read four books during my last vacation.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: v - a - c - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor where you went on your last vacation, with your helper listening for full sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The time that has not happened yet, coming after today.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In the future, I want to be a teacher.
practiceSentences: Amal draws a picture of her future classroom.
Nobody knows the future, so we plan carefully.
What job would you like in the future?
Adam said, "In the future I will build ships."
I save my coins for the future.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - u - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say one job you want in the future, then ask the tutor whether your sentence sounds clear.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: In a short time from now, with not long to wait.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will go on vacation soon.
practiceSentences: Teacher Yasmin says the results will arrive soon.
The bell will ring soon for break time.
Will the rain stop soon so we can play?
Adam said, "The fair is coming very soon!"
I will finish my chores soon and then read.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor for two more sentences using soon, then read them aloud to your helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Earlier than something else, coming before another time.
practiceSentences: March comes sooner than June on every calendar.
The bus arrived sooner than we expected today.
Which comes sooner, break time or lunch?
Maya said, "Finish your work sooner and rest longer."
I woke up sooner than my sleepy brother.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - o - o - n - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to explain the difference between soon and sooner while an adult listens with you.

rubrics.level1: Hard to hear, with many words unclear.
rubrics.level2: Sometimes clear, but the listener has to guess.
rubrics.level3: Mostly clear, with only one or two unclear words.
rubrics.level4: Clear and confident from the first sentence to the last.

rubrics.level1: Uses one or two unit words, often in the wrong place.
rubrics.level3: Uses five unit words correctly, including two time words.
rubrics.level4: Uses six or more unit words correctly and precisely.

rubrics.level1: Most sentences are incomplete or use the wrong tense.
rubrics.level2: About half the sentences are complete and correctly timed.
rubrics.level3: Most sentences are complete, with correct present and past forms.
rubrics.level4: Every sentence is complete, with present, past and future used correctly.

rubrics.level1: Answers only with single words when asked.
rubrics.level2: Answers questions, but never asks one back.
rubrics.level3: Answers fully and asks one question back.
rubrics.level4: Answers fully, asks questions back, and builds on what the partner said.

rubrics.level1: One idea, with no supporting detail.
rubrics.level2: Two or three ideas, with little detail.
rubrics.level3: Four ideas, each with one supporting detail.
rubrics.level4: Five or more ideas, each developed with precise detail.

rubrics.level2: Some sentences follow the order of the day or the year.
rubrics.level3: Ideas follow one clear time order throughout.
rubrics.level4: Ideas follow one clear time order and use openers such as First, Next and Then.

rubrics.level1: Verb tenses are mixed up throughout.
rubrics.level2: Present or past is correct in about half the sentences.
rubrics.level3: Present and past forms are correct in most sentences.
rubrics.level4: Present, past and future forms are correct, including irregular verbs.

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and full stops are mostly missing.
rubrics.level3: Most sentences start with capitals and end with full stops.
rubrics.level4: Every sentence is punctuated correctly and every month has its capital letter.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and spell the twelve months of the year, in order.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Word Wall ticked for all twelve months, and the Activity 1 month order completed correctly.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about your daily routine using the hours of the day.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Speaking 1 recording naming at least four hours, and the Writing 1 routine report.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the Present Simple to talk about what you do every day.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Grammar 1 practice answers, and the present-tense choices in Activity 3.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the Past Simple and irregular verbs to talk about what already happened.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Grammar 2 and Grammar 3 practice answers, and the Writing 2 vacation summary.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about the future and about what will happen soon.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The future sentence in Speaking 2, and the future item in Activity 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Compare things with -er and -est (comparatives and superlatives).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Grammar 4 practice answers, and one comparative sentence used in Writing 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask and answer Yes/No and Wh- questions about time.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Grammar 5 practice answers, and the questions asked during Speaking 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give simple instructions using imperatives (Prepare your calendar. Plan your day.).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Grammar 6 imperative answers, and the three instructions written for Writing 3.

readings.title: My Day, Hour by Hour
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: My name is Idris. I have a busy day. At 6 o’clock, I wake up and get ready. At 7, I eat breakfast with my family. At 8, I walk to school. We study for many hours. At 1 o’clock, we eat lunch. At 4, I go home and help my mother. In the evening, I read a book. Every day is a little different, but I always try to plan my time.

readings.title: The Twelve Months
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: A year has twelve months. The first month is January, and the last is December. Some months are hot and some are cool. Some months have rain and some are dry. In our school, the year begins in January and ends in November. Families use a calendar to remember special days, such as the new year, birthdays, and the days to plant and pick their crops.

readings.title: Making a Calendar
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “I made a calendar for the whole year. In June, we have school games. In August, we go on vacation to visit my grandma. In October, we pick mangoes. I marked every special month!”

readings.title: The Months Song
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sing along, and count the months on your fingers:
“January, February, March and April, May and June, July, August, September, October, November, December — twelve months make one year!”

readings.title: The Calendar on the Wall
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
One bright Monday morning, Amal sat in class and looked at the big calendar on the wall. Teacher Yasmin had just asked a question.
“When does the school year begin?”
“In January,” said Amal.
“Good,” said Teacher Yasmin. “And when does it end?”
“In November,” said Nora.
“Correct. Now let us each make a calendar that shows what we do each month.”
Amal liked the idea. She wrote her name at the top of her paper and drew a square for each month: January, February, March, and so on.
Then she thought about the future.
“In June, we have school games,” she wrote. “In August, we go on vacation to visit Grandma Hana.”
That night, at home, she showed the calendar to her grandmother.
“In October, we pick mangoes,” said Grandma Hana, smiling. “And in December, the weather is hot.”
“What happened in the old days, Grandma Hana?” asked Amal.
“Oh, a lot,” she said. “A century ago, people did not have printed calendars. They watched the sky, the moon, and the trees to know the time of year.”
Amal’s eyes grew wide. “A hundred years ago?”
“Yes,” Grandma Hana laughed. “A century sounds long, but it goes faster than you think.”
The next day at school, Teacher Yasmin asked, “Did you prepare your calendar?”
“Yes,” said Amal. “And I asked Grandma Hana about the past.”
“Great,” said Teacher Yasmin. “Now add some details. What happens each hour of your day?”
Amal wrote: “At 6 o’clock, I wake up. At 7, I eat breakfast. At 8, I walk to school. At 1, we eat lunch. At 4, I go home and help with the chores.”
Then she thought about the future again. “When I grow up,” she wrote, “I want to be a teacher. I will help students plan their day and prepare for the next century.”
Later in the week, the school had a quiz.
“What month comes sooner — April or May?” asked Teacher Yasmin.
“April,” said Amal. “It comes sooner.”
“And what will happen soon?” asked Teacher Yasmin.
“Our vacation!” the class shouted together.
At the end of the week, parents visited the school. Amal showed her calendar to her mother.
“Wow,” said her mother. “You even marked the days we plant maize.”
Amal smiled. “I want to remember everything.”
Her mother added, “That is smart. The calendar helps us plan.”
On the last page of her book, Amal wrote a short poem:
The months go fast, the hours too.
The future is coming, it’s all brand new.
Look at the past, remember it well,
Time is a story we all must tell.

comprehension.question: What does Idris do at 6 o’clock? What does he do at 1 o’clock?
comprehension.correctAnswer: At 6 o’clock he wakes up and gets ready. At 1 o’clock he eats lunch.
comprehension.explanation: The text sets out Idris’s day hour by hour, so both times are stated directly.

comprehension.question: Idris names two meals in his day. Which meals are they, and when does he eat them?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He eats breakfast at 7 o’clock and lunch at 1 o’clock.
comprehension.explanation: Only breakfast and lunch are given times; the evening is spent reading, not eating.

comprehension.question: In ‘The Twelve Months’, which month is named first and which is named last?
comprehension.correctAnswer: January is named first and December is named last.
comprehension.explanation: The text states that the first month is January and the last is December.

comprehension.question: The school year does not fill the whole calendar. Between which two months does it run?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It runs from January to November.
comprehension.explanation: The text says the school year begins in January and ends in November, so December sits outside it.

comprehension.question: The speaker marks three special months. Name each month and say what happens in it.
comprehension.correctAnswer: She has school games in June and a family vacation in August. In October she picks mangoes with her family.
comprehension.explanation: All three months and their events are listed in order in the short listening text.

comprehension.question: In ‘The Months Song’, which month is sung after September? Which month is sung before June?
comprehension.correctAnswer: October is sung after September, and May is sung before June.
comprehension.explanation: The song lists the months in order, so each month’s neighbours can be heard directly.

comprehension.question: What task does Teacher Yasmin set the class once Amal has answered her question?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She asks every learner to make a calendar showing what they do each month.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin says: Now let us each make a calendar that shows what we do each month.

comprehension.question: Grandma Hana says people had no printed calendars a century ago. What did they watch instead of one?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They watched the sky, the moon and the trees.
comprehension.explanation: Grandma Hana names all three natural signs when she explains how people once told the time of year.

comprehension.question: Amal writes what she does at five different times of day. List three of them with the hour.
comprehension.correctAnswer: At 6 she wakes up and at 7 she eats breakfast. At 8 she walks to school and at 1 they eat lunch.
comprehension.explanation: Any three of the five times Amal wrote down are correct, taken straight from her written plan.

comprehension.question: How does Amal feel when she learns that a century is one hundred years? Which words in the story show this?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She feels amazed, and the words that show it are: Amal’s eyes grew wide.
comprehension.explanation: The story never names the feeling, so the reader uses the description of her eyes as the evidence.

comprehension.question: Grandma Hana says that a century goes faster than you think. What is she telling Amal about time?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She means that long stretches of time pass more quickly than we expect.
comprehension.explanation: This meaning is implied rather than stated, so the answer has to be worked out from what she says.

comprehension.question: Amal says that April comes sooner than May. What does the word sooner tell us about the two months?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It tells us that April arrives earlier in the year than May does.
comprehension.explanation: Sooner compares two points in time and marks the one that happens first.

grammar.title: Present Simple
grammar.explanation: We use the Present Simple to talk about things that happen again and again, and about facts that are always true. It is the “routine tense”. When we say “She marks the calendar every morning,” we mean she does it regularly. This helps us describe our daily life clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the subject: I, you, he, she, it, we, they.
Use the base verb (the verb with no “to”): mark, play, go.
For he, she, it, add s or es: “She marks the calendar.” “He watches the clock.”
Negative: use do not / does not + base verb: “She does not play football.”
Question: start with Do / Does: “Does he go to school at 8 o’clock?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She mark the calendar.” ✗
Fix: For she, add the “s”: “She marks the calendar.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the Present Simple as the routine tense. He, she and it never forget their “s”!
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the Present Simple form of the verb in brackets. | My brother ______ (play) games every weekend. | The cat ______ (chase) the mouse in the garden. | We ______ (not eat) junk food. | ______ (she / read) a book every night? | The train ______ (leave) at 9 o’clock.

grammar.title: Past Simple
grammar.explanation: We use the Past Simple to talk about things that already happened at a certain time in the past. It helps us tell stories and share what we did. When we say “We went on vacation in August,” we are talking about the past.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For most verbs, add -ed to the base verb: play → played, visit → visited.
Some verbs are irregular and change fully: go → went, see → saw (see the next lesson).
Negative: use did not (didn’t) + base verb: “I didn’t see the calendar.”
Question: start with Did + subject + base verb: “Did you see the calendar?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She finish her homework yesterday.” ✗
Fix: Add -ed for regular verbs: “She finished her homework yesterday.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: The Past Simple is a time machine. To travel back, add “-ed” to regular verbs — and learn the special irregular ones by heart.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the Past Simple form of the verb in brackets. | Last year, we ______ (go) to the beach. | I ______ (see) a good film last weekend. | They ______ (play) in the park yesterday. | She ______ (visit) her friend last month.

grammar.title: Irregular Past Verbs
grammar.explanation: Most verbs add “-ed” in the past, but some special verbs change in their own way. These are irregular past verbs. We must learn them by heart, because they do not follow the “-ed” rule.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Change the whole verb to its special past form. Some common ones:
go → went: “They went on vacation.”
see → saw: “She saw the calendar.”
eat → ate: “He ate his lunch quickly.”
have → had: “We had a great time.”
take → took: “I took my dog for a walk.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “They goed to the store.” ✗
Fix: “go” is irregular, so it becomes went: “They went to the store.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Make flashcards: write the present verb on one side (go) and the past on the other (went). Practise with a friend or your AI tutor.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the correct irregular past verb from the word bank: went, saw, ate, had, took. | Yesterday, I ______ my homework out of my bag. | They ______ to the park last week. | She ______ a delicious sandwich for lunch. | We ______ a great time at the sports day. | He ______ the film last night.

grammar.title: Comparatives and Superlatives
grammar.explanation: We use comparatives to compare two things (bigger, warmer). We use superlatives to talk about the best or the most of three or more things (the biggest, the warmest). This helps us describe and compare clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short adjectives — comparative adds -er, superlative adds -est: warm → warmer → the warmest.
Adjectives ending in -y — change y to i: happy → happier → the happiest.
Long adjectives — use more and the most: beautiful → more beautiful → the most beautiful.
Irregular: good → better → the best; bad → worse → the worst.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He is more tall than his brother.” ✗
Fix: “tall” is short, so add “-er”: “He is taller than his brother.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think: “-er” is one step up (comparing two), and “-est” is the top of the steps (comparing many). “June is warmer than April; July is the warmest.”
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the correct form of the adjective in brackets. | My sister is ______ (smart) than I am. | This puzzle is the ______ (hard) one I have ever tried. | A cheetah is ______ (fast) than a lion. | This is the ______ (funny) film I have seen all year.

grammar.title: Yes/No and Wh- Questions
grammar.explanation: We ask questions to find out things. Yes/No questions can be answered with yes or no (“Did you miss the lesson?”). Wh- questions ask for more information and begin with who, what, when, where, why or how (“When is your vacation?”).
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Yes/No: Helping verb + subject + main verb? “Did you miss the lesson?”
Helping verbs include: do, does, did, is, are, was, were, can, will.
Wh-: Wh- word + helping verb + subject + main verb? “When did you go?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Your vacation when is in September?” ✗
Fix: Put the words in order — Wh- word first: “When is your vacation in September?” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Remember the pattern: for Yes/No questions, “Helping verb + subject + main verb?”. For Wh- questions, add the Wh- word at the very front.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct word to complete each question. | ______ (Do / Did) you enjoy the film? (it is over now) | ______ (What / Where) is your favourite book? | ______ (Can / Will) you help me with my project, please? | ______ (Who / When) is your best friend?

grammar.title: Imperatives (Giving Instructions) and Pronouns
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are sentences that give a command, an instruction or a request, so we reach for them whenever we explain how to do something. Pronouns are the small words we use in place of a noun, so we do not repeat the same name again and again.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Imperative: start with the base verb. Prepare your calendar. Plan your day.
No subject — we do not say “you”. Not “You clean your room”, just “Clean your room.”
Negative imperative: add do not / don’t: “Don’t be late!”
Polite imperative: add please: “Please listen carefully.”
Pronouns: I for yourself; he for one boy or man; she for one girl or woman; it for one thing, animal or idea; we for you and others; they for more than one.
Instead of “Maya is an artist. Maya loves to paint,” we say “Maya is an artist. She loves to paint.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Preparing your calendar.” and “Her is my friend.” ✗
Fix: Use the base verb for an imperative and the subject pronoun at the front: “Prepare your calendar.” and “She is my friend.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: An imperative never carries a “you” in front of it — just say the action. For pronouns, find the noun first, then swap it: one girl → she, one thing → it, one group → they.
grammar.practice: Part A: fill in the blank with the imperative (base) form of the verb in brackets. | ______ your homework before dinner. (finish) | ______ the plants every day. (water) | ______ your shoes before you enter. (take off) | ______ the table for lunch. (set) | ______ your teacher when you have a question. (ask) | Part B: choose the correct pronoun to complete each sentence. | ______ am going to the market. (I / They) | Nora is my best friend. ______ is very kind. (She / He) | The cat is sleeping. ______ looks so cute! (It / They) | Leo and I are friends. ______ like to play football. (We / He) | The girls are laughing. ______ are having fun. (They / She)

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — My Daily Routine
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to tell your AI tutor about your day. Use the hours:
“At ______ o’clock, I ______.”
“In the morning, I ______.”
“In the evening, I ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say at least four things? Did you use the hours of the day? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult, tell the tutor four things you do at set hours, then ask which sentence was clearest.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Past, Present and Future
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Say one sentence for each time:
Past: “Last vacation, I ______.” (use a past verb, like went or visited)
Present: “Every day, I ______.”
Future: “In the future, I want to ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper listening, say one past, one present and one future sentence, then ask the tutor to name each tense.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — When Is It?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person names something, such as a birthday, the school fair, or the school games.
The other person asks: “When is it?”
Answer with a month: “It is in ______.” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to play When Is It with you and your helper, and to correct any month you say wrongly.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose six unit words: calendar, hour, century, vacation, future and sooner.
Ask your partner one clear question about each word, such as “How many minutes are in one hour?”
Then answer their six questions back, always in complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to answer your six word questions, with an adult checking that you speak in complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Amal’s mother said, “The calendar helps us plan.”
Explain in your own words how a calendar helps your family plan the year.
Give one reason using because, then answer two follow-up questions from your listener.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Give your reason to the tutor while a grown-up listens, then ask for two harder follow-up questions.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Plan and record one-minute talk called My Favourite Month.
Open by naming the month, give three reasons why you like it, and use one comparing word such as warmer or busiest.
Close by saying what your family does in that month each year.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Record your talk with an adult present, then ask the tutor to score your opening, your details and your closing.

writing.title: Writing 1 — My Daily Routine (Information Report)
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four or five sentences about what you do each day. Use the hours and the Present Simple:
At 6 o’clock, I ______.
At 7, I ______.
At 8, I ______.
In the evening, I ______.
writing.modelText: At 6 o’clock, I wake up and wash. At 7, I eat breakfast with my family. At 8, I walk to school with Nora. In the evening, I read for one hour before bed.
writing.successCriteria: I used the hour in every sentence; I kept every verb in the Present Simple; I put my day in time order; I checked capitals and full stops.
writing.support: Say the whole day aloud first, then write one sentence for each hour you named.
writing.extension: Add two more hours from your afternoon, and finish with one sentence about the part you like best.

writing.title: Writing 2 — My Last Vacation (Summary)
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about your last vacation. Use the Past Simple. Answer: When did it happen? Where did you go? What did you do?
Example: “Last August, we went to visit my grandma. We picked mangoes and played games. It was one happy vacation.”
writing.modelText: Last August, we went to visit Grandma Hana. We picked mangoes and played games in the yard. It was my happiest vacation so far.
writing.successCriteria: I named the month; I used past verbs throughout; I included one irregular past verb such as went or saw; I reread my work once.
writing.support: List your three answers as notes before you turn each note into one full sentence.
writing.extension: Add one sentence comparing this vacation with an earlier one, using a word ending in -er.

writing.title: Writing 3 — How to Make a Calendar (Instructions)
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three instructions that tell someone how to make a calendar. Start each one with a command word (an imperative). See the Imperatives grammar lesson.
Example: “First, draw twelve squares. Next, write one month in each square. Then, mark your special days.”
writing.modelText: First, draw twelve squares on one large sheet. Next, write one month inside each square. Then, mark your birthday and your school games.
writing.successCriteria: Every instruction begins with the base verb; my steps run in the right order; I used First, Next and Then; there is no word you at the start.
writing.support: Make your own calendar first, then write down what you did at each step.
writing.extension: Number your steps and add one polite instruction that begins with Please.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write one six-sentence paragraph about how you plan your week. Begin with a topic sentence, then join your ideas with and, but or because. Include one comparing word such as busier or busiest.
writing.modelText: I plan my week on our kitchen calendar because it helps me remember everything. On Monday I have reading practice, but on Friday I have football. Saturday is my busiest day of all.
writing.successCriteria: My first sentence tells the reader my topic; I joined ideas with and, but or because; I used one -er or -est word; my paragraph stays on one subject.
writing.support: Write your six ideas in a list, then join the ones that belong together.
writing.extension: Turn two of your sentences into one longer sentence using because.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write the heading The Twelve Months, then five true facts about the year. Use at least three unit words and check that every month begins with a capital letter.
writing.modelText: The Twelve Months. One year has twelve months. January is the first month. December is the last month. February is the shortest month. Our school year ends in November.
writing.successCriteria: I wrote the heading; all five facts are true; I used three or more unit words; every month name starts with the capital letter.
writing.support: Check each fact against the reading The Twelve Months before you write it down.
writing.extension: Add one fact about hours or centuries, and say where you found it.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Reread your Writing 1 routine. Rewrite three sentences with better time openers, add two new details, then explain in one sentence what you changed and why.
writing.modelText: In my first draft I wrote, “I eat breakfast.” Now I write, “At 7 o’clock, I eat breakfast with Idris.” I added the hour because it tells the reader much more.
writing.sentenceStarter: In my first draft, I wrote
writing.successCriteria: I rewrote three sentences; each new opener names the time; I added two real details; I explained one change in my own words.
writing.support: Underline the three weakest sentences in your draft before you rewrite them.
writing.extension: Swap with your partner and suggest one further improvement for their work.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Put the Months in Order
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write these months in the correct order: May, January, March, April, February, June.
Check that each month keeps its capital letter.
activities.answerSummary: The six months belong in calendar order from January through to June, each with the capital letter.

activities.title: Activities 2 — True or False (Time Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each fact. If it is false, write the correct answer.
There are 12 months in a year.
There are 60 minutes in one hour.
A century is 10 years.
September comes sooner than November.
activities.answerSummary: Three facts are true; only the century fact is false, and it should read one hundred years.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Past or Present?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the correct verb for each sentence.
Every morning, she ______ (marks / marked) the calendar.
Last week, we ______ (go / went) on vacation.
They ______ (play / played) football yesterday.
He ______ (walks / walked) to school every day.
activities.answerSummary: Time words decide the tense: every morning and every day take the present, last week and yesterday take the past.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Finish each sentence about yourself.
Today’s month is ______.
My favourite month is ______, because ______.
There are ______ months in a year.
In the future, I want to ______.
activities.answerSummary: Three answers are personal and one is fixed: every year has twelve months.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Sort these twelve words into three groups: months, time words, and words that compare time.
Words: March, May, July, September, December, calendar, hour, century, vacation, future, soon, sooner.
Write one sentence explaining the rule you used for each group.
activities.answerSummary: Five month names, five time nouns and two comparing adverbs, each group explained in the learner’s own sentence.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Find one sentence about time in each of the five unit texts.
Copy the sentence, name the text it came from, and explain what it tells the reader.
activities.answerSummary: One quoted sentence per text, correctly attributed, with the learner’s explanation of what it shows.

assignments.title: Time and Daily Life learning portfolio
assignments.instructions:
Submit two pieces of work. First, your Writing 1 daily routine, written in the Present Simple with the hour named in every sentence. Second, your Speaking 1 recording, saying at least four routine sentences clearly.
Marking guidance: the writing is marked out of 16 on the Writing rubric (Ideas, Organisation, Language, Conventions) and the recording out of 16 on the Speaking rubric (Clarity, Vocabulary, Sentence accuracy, Interaction), giving 32 marks in total. Award full marks for Conventions only when every month name carries its capital letter.
Improve both pieces once using your teacher’s feedback before you upload them.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does the word calendar mean?
quizzes.options: A short rest from school or work. | A chart showing the days, weeks and months of one year. | A length of time that lasts sixty minutes. | A period of one hundred years that historians like to write about.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A chart showing the days, weeks and months of one year.
quizzes.explanation: A calendar is the chart itself. The other three describe a vacation, an hour and a century.

quizzes.question: How many months does one full year have?
quizzes.options: Ten months. | Seven months. | Twelve months. | One hundred months altogether.
quizzes.explanation: The reading The Twelve Months states it directly: one year has twelve months.

quizzes.question: In ‘The Twelve Months’, when does the school year begin and end?
quizzes.options: It begins in January and ends in November. | It begins in March and ends in December. | It begins in June and ends in August. | It begins in September and finishes at the end of the following June.
quizzes.correctAnswer: It begins in January and ends in November.
quizzes.explanation: The text says the school year begins in January and ends in November, so December falls outside it.

quizzes.question: Which month is sung straight after September in ‘The Months Song’?
quizzes.options: August. | October. | November. | December, the very last month of the year.
quizzes.explanation: The song runs September, October, November, December, so October follows September.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing month from the story. “What month comes sooner — _____ or May?” asked Teacher Yasmin.
quizzes.explanation: Teacher Yasmin compares the two months either side of each other, and Amal answers April.

quizzes.question: What does the word century mean?
quizzes.options: A period of one hundred years. | A time when you stop school to rest. | The time that has not happened yet. | A chart on the wall that helps a family plan the whole year.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A period of one hundred years.
quizzes.explanation: Grandma Hana uses century to mean one hundred years. The others describe a vacation, the future and a calendar.

quizzes.question: How many minutes make one hour?
quizzes.options: Twenty-four. | One hundred. | Sixty. | Three hundred and sixty-five.
quizzes.explanation: An hour is sixty minutes. Twenty-four is the hours in one day and three hundred and sixty-five is the days in one year.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the Past Simple correctly?
quizzes.options: Last year we goed to the beach. | Last year we go to the beach. | Last year we are going to the beach very early in the morning. | Last year we went to the beach.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Last year we went to the beach.
quizzes.explanation: Go is irregular, so its past form is went. Goed does not exist, and the other two are present forms.

quizzes.question: Which one of these sentences is an imperative?
quizzes.options: I prepare my calendar every Sunday. | Prepare your calendar. | She prepared her calendar last night. | Are you preparing your calendar this evening?
quizzes.explanation: An imperative starts with the base verb and has no subject in front of it.

quizzes.question: In Amal’s poem, which line says that time is something everyone must share?
quizzes.options: The months go fast, the hours too. | The future is coming, it’s all brand new. | Look at the past, remember it well. | Time is a story we all must tell.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Time is a story we all must tell.
quizzes.explanation: The closing line calls time a story we all must tell, which makes it something shared by everyone.

liveSessions.title: Time and Daily Life: Launch, vocabulary and learning goals
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the first six months from Vocabulary Group 1.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min months chant; 10 min teacher models the calendar-square task; 15 min learners build their January to June squares; 10 min partner quiz on month order; 5 min goal setting
liveSessions.afterSession: Learn the remaining six months before the next session.

liveSessions.title: Time and Daily Life: Reading fluency and comprehension
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read ‘My Day, Hour by Hour’ and ‘The Twelve Months’ twice aloud.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min hour-by-hour retrieval; 10 min teacher reads the story opening with expression; 15 min paired reading of the story; 10 min evidence hunt across the two information texts; 5 min exit question
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer comprehension questions one to six in writing.

liveSessions.title: Time and Daily Life: Grammar workshop and feedback
liveSessions.beforeSession: Work through Grammar lessons one to three and bring your written answers.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min verb sort; 10 min teacher models the -s ending and the -ed ending; 15 min irregular verb card game; 10 min learners correct four wrong sentences; 5 min feedback round
liveSessions.afterSession: Redo any grammar item you got wrong and explain the fix.

liveSessions.title: Time and Daily Life: Dialogue, speaking and pronunciation
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare four routine sentences and one past sentence for Speaking 1 and Speaking 2.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min months chant with clapping; 10 min teacher models one routine talk; 15 min learners record Speaking 1 in pairs; 10 min When Is It game; 5 min peer praise
liveSessions.afterSession: Upload your Speaking 1 recording and listen back to it once.

liveSessions.title: Time and Daily Life: Writing conference and revision
liveSessions.beforeSession: Draft Writing 1 and Writing 3 and bring both drafts to the session.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min capital-letter check; 10 min teacher models an instruction text; 15 min individual writing conferences; 10 min partners swap and suggest one improvement; 5 min revision plan
liveSessions.afterSession: Rewrite three sentences using the improvement your partner suggested.

liveSessions.title: Time and Daily Life: Performance, assessment and reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Practise your one-minute unit talk and revise the ten quiz topics.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min quick-fire month questions; 10 min three learners present their unit talk; 15 min unit quiz; 10 min self-assessment and portfolio check; 5 min preview of the next unit
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the unit quiz and self-assessment, then submit your portfolio.

teacherNotes.note: Model each new time word aloud before independent work, drill month spellings and capital letters at the board, and give specific feedback on complete sentences and text evidence. Learners who cannot yet order twelve months should work with the first six only. Device safety: an adult must stay present for every AI tutor session, and no child should be left alone with the device.

💬 Ask Your AI Tutor (Reading and listening)
“Can you read the two passages with me and explain any hard words?”
“Can you play a listening game about the months of the year?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor (Speaking)
“Can you help me practise talking about my daily routine?”
“Can you play the ‘When Is It?’ game with me?”
“Can you check that I used the past and future correctly?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor (Writing)
“Can you check if my sentences are correct?”
“Did I begin every month with a capital letter?”
“Can you help me make my instructions clear and in order?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor (Vocabulary)
“Can you quiz me on the months in order?”
“How do I say and spell this month?”
“Which months are hot and which months have rain where I live?”
“What comes after October? What comes before September?”
“Can you help me use the word ‘century’ in a sentence?”
“What is the difference between an hour and a day?”
“What is the difference between ‘soon’ and ‘sooner’?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor (Grammar)
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘she likes’ but ‘I like’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise the Past Simple?”
Every one of these sessions runs with an adult sitting alongside the child.
teacherNotes.sourceFile: Grade 3 Lesson, Vocabulary, Story and Grammar source

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: January, February, March, April, May, June. Each month keeps its capital letter, and June is the sixth month.
1. True, there are twelve. 2. True, sixty minutes make one hour. 3. False, a century is one hundred years, not ten. 4. True, September is the ninth month and November the eleventh.
1. marks, because every morning signals a routine. 2. went, the irregular past of go. 3. played, regular past with -ed. 4. walks, because every day signals a routine.
1. Accept today’s real month, capitalised. 2. Accept any month followed by a genuine reason after because. 3. twelve. 4. Accept any future plan expressed with want to plus a verb.

Months: March, May, July, September, December. Time words: calendar, hour, century, vacation, future. Comparing words: soon, sooner. Accept any rule sentence that names the shared feature of the group.

One sentence per text, for example: Idris studies for many hours; one year has twelve months; the speaker picks mangoes in October; the song ends with December; Grandma Hana watched the sky, the moon and the trees.
A chart showing the days, weeks and months of one year. Point out that vacation, hour and century are the three distractors.
Twelve months. Ask the learner to count them aloud from January to December as proof.
It begins in January and ends in November. December is the one month that falls outside this school year.
October. Have the learner sing the sequence September, October, November to confirm it.
April. April is the fourth month and May the fifth, so April arrives first in the year.
A period of one hundred years. Link it back to Grandma Hana saying people had no printed calendars a century ago.
Sixty. Twenty-four counts the hours in one day, and three hundred and sixty-five counts the days in one year.
Last year we went to the beach. Went is the irregular past of go; goed is never a word.
Prepare your calendar. It begins with the base verb and carries no subject, which is what makes it an imperative.
Time is a story we all must tell. The words we all make time something everyone shares.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the twelve months in order.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about my daily routine using the hours.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the Present Simple for things I do every day.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the Past Simple to talk about what happened.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about the future and what will happen soon.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can compare things with -er and -est.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
